# Bike Demand Forecasting with Calibrated Uncertainty

Day-ahead hourly bike rental demand forecast with 90%-target
split-conformal prediction intervals using the UCI Bike Sharing dataset.

<!-- RESULTS:START -->
## Key Results

*Results are autogenerated from the last experiment run.*

**Data source:** UCI Bike Sharing
**Selected method:** random_forest (by calibration MASE)
**Test samples:** 4257

| Metric | seasonal_naive | ridge | random_forest | hist_gradient_boosting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MASE** | 1.4852 | 1.1111 | 1.1543 | 1.2352 |
| **MAE** | 79.7839 | 59.6843 | 62.0085 | 66.3512 |
| **RMSE** | 133.2734 | 93.2630 | 97.9747 | 102.6507 |
| **SMAPE** | 41.9292 | 39.2748 | 32.3037 | 41.4869 |

### Prediction Intervals (90% target coverage)

| Metric | seasonal_naive | ridge | random_forest | hist_gradient_boosting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Interval Coverage** | 90.9% | 89.5% | 89.2% | 88.3% |
| **Mean Interval Width** | 395.07 | 262.21 | 281.70 | 285.25 |
| **Winkler Score** | 615.67 | 431.94 | 459.38 | 473.71 |

**Coverage caveat:** The following methods fell below the 90% empirical coverage target: ridge, random_forest, hist_gradient_boosting. Chronological calibration and test residuals are not guaranteed to be exchangeable; treat these intervals as experimental uncertainty estimates, not coverage guarantees.

### Paired MASE Improvement vs Seasonal Naive (95% bootstrap CI)

| Method | Mean improvement | 95% CI | Probability of improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| ridge | 0.3637 | [0.2390, 0.4892] | 100.0% |
| random_forest | 0.3238 | [0.2061, 0.4435] | 100.0% |
| hist_gradient_boosting | 0.2389 | [0.1055, 0.3739] | 100.0% |

<!-- RESULTS:END -->

## Why This Project Matters

Bike-share operators must schedule bicycle rebalancing and staffing on a
day-ahead basis. Accurate demand forecasts with reliable uncertainty
quantification enable efficient resource allocation, reducing both
empty-station events and wasted staff/repositioning costs.

This project provides:

- Point forecasts for the next 24 hours (hourly).
- Split-conformal prediction intervals with a 90% target coverage level.
- Rigorous evaluation with proper leakage prevention.
- Reproducible comparison of a seasonal naive baseline against Ridge,
  Random Forest, and HistGradientBoosting alternatives.

## Project Architecture

```
Raw hourly data (UCI) --> Reindex to complete hourly grid
  --> Feature engineering (calendar, lags, rolling summaries)
  --> Chronological train/cal/test split
  --> [Seasonal naive, Ridge, Random Forest, HistGradientBoosting]
  --> Split-conformal intervals
  --> Metrics, figures, report
```

Key design decisions:

- **Strict leakage prevention:** casual/registered columns and target-hour
  weather variables are excluded. Data is reindexed before time shifts.
- **Chronological split:** no random shuffle. Ensures temporal validity.
- **MASE primary metric:** seasonal scale computed on training demand only.
- **Day-block bootstrap:** for paired MASE improvement confidence intervals.

## Dataset

**UCI Bike Sharing dataset** (dataset id 275).

- **Source:** UCI Machine Learning Repository
- **DOI:** 10.24432/C5W894
- **License:** CC BY 4.0
- **Records:** 17,389 hourly records from 2011-01-01 to 2012-12-31
- **Location:** Washington, D.C., USA

See [docs/data_card.md](docs/data_card.md) for full documentation.

Download the data:

```bash
python scripts/download_data.py
```

## Methodology

Four methods are compared on the same feature set and temporal splits:

1. **Seasonal Naive** -- Predict using value from 24 hours earlier (lag-24).
2. **Ridge Regression** -- Linear model with L2 regularisation.
3. **Random Forest** -- Ensemble of decision trees.
4. **HistGradientBoosting** -- Gradient-boosted tree ensemble (scikit-learn).

All predictions are clipped at zero. The best method by calibration MASE is
selected for interval plotting; all test metrics are reported.

- **Conformal intervals:** Split-conformal absolute residual quantile with
  finite-sample correction and 90% target coverage. Chronological residuals
  are not guaranteed to be exchangeable, so empirical coverage is reported
  and intervals are not presented as coverage guarantees.
- **Validation:** Chronological train/cal/test split (60/15/25) at day
  boundaries. MASE computed with seasonal scale on training data only.
- **Uncertainty:** Day-block bootstrap 95% CI for paired MASE improvement
  (each trained method vs seasonal naive).
- **Leakage prevention:** cnt, casual, registered columns and target-hour
  weather variables are strictly excluded. Data is reindexed to complete
  hourly grid before time-based shifts.

See [docs/methodology.md](docs/methodology.md) for equations.

## Installation

```bash
# With uv (recommended)
uv sync --extra dev
```

## Reproduce the Results

```bash
# Download data first
python scripts/download_data.py

# Full evaluation
python scripts/reproduce.py

# Smoke test (synthetic data, CI-safe)
python scripts/reproduce.py --smoke
```

Artifacts are written to ``reports/`` (full) or ``reports/smoke/`` (smoke).

## Tests and Quality Checks

```bash
make test        # pytest with branch coverage (>=80%)
make lint        # ruff check
make format      # ruff format
make type-check  # mypy (blocking)
make security    # bandit (blocking)
make smoke       # smoke pipeline
make quality     # all checks combined (all blocking)
```

## Results

Generated results appear in ``reports/metrics/summary.json`` and
``reports/final_report.md`` after running the experiment.

## Error Analysis

The report includes per-hour error breakdowns and three figures:

- **model_comparison.png:** MASE and MAE bar chart for all four methods.
- **error_by_hour.png:** MAE by hour of day for all four methods.
- **prediction_intervals.png:** Sample of test predictions with intervals for the calibration-selected method.

## Limitations

1. **Limited dataset:** Two years from one city (Washington, D.C.).
2. **No weather forecast integration:** Uses only lag and calendar features.
3. **Simple conformal method:** Absolute residual quantile assumes exchangeability.
4. **Limited methods.** Four methods are compared; additional methods
   (SARIMA, LightGBM, neural networks) could provide broader insight.
5. **Offline evaluation:** Metrics on historical data only.

See [docs/limitations.md](docs/limitations.md) for a full list.
